articles.title: LEAVING
articles.summary: The era of "brand forgiveness" is over, especially for the customers you can't afford to lose. New global research from the Oliver Wyman Forum shows 69% of high-income consumers will switch brands after a single bad experience.

articles.title: THE CREATOR ADVANTAGE 2026
articles.summary: Using the VIT framework (Volume, Frequency, Impact, and Trust), brands now prioritize relevance over raw numbers. While most industries saw performance declines despite hiring more influencers, Beauty stood out with a 22% VIT increase by focusing on retention. Smaller voices are driving this shift. Nano-creators saw a 50% VIT surge in high-intent actions like saves. They're moving away from cookie-cutter posts toward authentic, creator-led systems that reward consistency.

articles.title: SHORT SUPER BOWL AD TEASERS MISS THE EMOTIONAL MARK, SYSTEM1 ANALYSIS FINDS
articles.summary: Short Super Bowl ad teasers create weaker emotional impact than longer ads and reduce campaign effectiveness. Analysis across six years shows teaser spots score about 0.5 Stars lower than full length ads because viewers have less time to connect and build memory. The research finds that 20 to 40 second ads perform best, and brands should start Super Bowl campaigns in January and continue running ads after the game to extend impact. A 30 second Super Bowl ad now costs about $8 million, so it is important for a brand to run longer pre game ads and keep top performers in rotation, as seen in a 5.9 Star rated Lay's ad that still airs a year later.

articles.title: THE PERFECT PITCH: WHAT MILLIONS OF EMAILS TAUGHT US ABOUT PITCHING JOURNALISTS
articles.summary: Short email pitches drive the best journalist engagement. Emails with 100 to 300 word bodies get the highest open rates and reply rates, while emails under 300 words earn the most replies. About 50% of pitches paste a full press release into the email body, which is mainly a UK practice, while US pitches more often use short plain text. Personalization varies in depth, as 83% use the journalist's name but only 57% reference past articles, and shallow flattery often fails. Relevant connections to prior coverage work better. Strong pitches usually start with a timely stat or trend hook, give a clear headline takeaway, include about four bolded bullet points, and add expert quotes that explain why the findings matter instead of repeating the data.

articles.title: DINNERS ARE THE NEW TRADE SHOWS
articles.summary: Dinners are becoming more scalable than sponsoring trade shows because they're more targeted. They should be treated as a core channel, not just a social outing. Apply the same strategic rigor and high-quality data to these events as you would to any other GTM motion.

articles.title: 👻 IS AI SEARCH GHOSTING YOUR BRAND? FIND OUT HOW TO FIX IT
articles.summary: ChatGPT, Gemini, and AI Overviews are where your highest-intent customers search. If your brand isn't mentioned, you're not even in the race. Evertune [9] monitors how AI sees your brand, identifies the sites that shape AI responses, and prioritizes them for your teams to

articles.title: target. Get your site target list
3 TOOLS TO SPY ON COMPETITORS
articles.summary: Set alerts for any company on CompetitorTrack. Their emails will show up to your inbox automatically. SendView tracks trends across all your competitors. It also allows you to visually analyze their programs and campaigns. Owletter archives competitor newsletters, making it easy to go back months and compare campaigns and track patterns.

articles.title: HOW TO USE CLAUDE & GAMMA TO MAKE A DECK
articles.summary: To make slides with Claude, go to claude.ai and connect Gamma and Google Drive via "Connectors". Then prompt Claude to create a deck, pulling relevant Drive docs and web graphics. Answer a few questions about format, tone, and length, and edit heavily through Gamma if needed. For branded slides, skip Gamma's default templates, create your own style in Gamma. Save fonts, colors, and logo placement. Then prompt Claude to use that style so every deck reflects your brand.

articles.title: WHAT MAKES A NEWSLETTER WORTH OPENING?
articles.summary: Beehiiv's State of Newsletters 2026 report highlights that 28B emails were sent in 2025, yet engagement increased, reinforcing newsletters as a reliable owned channel. Readers favor concise subject lines, straightforward “here's what you need to know” framing, and strong opening sentences that justify the click. Consistency in send time and format builds habit, while misleading urgency language and surprise paywalls actively hurt performance.

articles.title: THE “MOORE-ISH” LAW OF MARKETING
articles.summary: Marketing is shifting from campaign launches to continuous systems as the cost and speed of experimentation collapse. AI reduces the effort to generate and deploy variations, pushing advantage toward teams that can measure results and make fast decisions. Content moves from single assets to always-on streams built from modular components, while personalization shifts from static segments to real-time situations like objections or constraints. Winning teams focus less on producing volume and more on building pipelines that test and learn based on compounding insights.

articles.title: LIGHT BEER WAS A FLOP, UNTIL THE RIGHT ADVERTISING CAME ALONG
articles.summary: Early light beers failed not because of taste or formulation, but because marketing framed them around dieting rather than enjoyment. Miller Lite broke through in the 1970s by repositioning light beer as less filling with great taste, using sports figures and barroom humor instead of health language. That shift created a mass-market category that later powered brands like Bud Light and Michelob Ultra to category leadership.

articles.title: WHAT WE LEARNED ABOUT AI SEARCH OPTIMIZATION
articles.summary: YouTube mentions are the strongest driver of AI search visibility, outperforming backlinks and domain authority.

articles.title: VAGUEPOSTING: WHY 2026 CONTENT IS ALL ABOUT KEEPING YOU CONFUSED
articles.summary: Vagueposting uses ambiguous language for the sake of generating controversy.
